key_departures.impact: 10-year franchise LT requested and received release after contract restructure talks collapsed. Detroit asked him to take a pay cut; he declined. His release saves $11.6M in cap space but creates a gaping hole at left tackle — the team's most troublesome unit in 2025. Penei Sewell could slide left, but that then opens a hole at RT. Either way, the Lions now need a starting-caliber tackle.

Released to save $5.5M in cap space. Glasgow had struggled mightily in 2025 after being moved from guard to center following Frank Ragnow's retirement, contributing to the Lions' 31st-ranked pass block win rate. His departure was necessary for cap reasons and created a pressing need at center that the Lions immediately moved to address.

Traded to Houston for IOL Juice Scruggs, a 2026 4th-round pick, and a 2027 7th-round pick. Montgomery was the power-back complement to Jahmyr Gibbs. His departure leaves Sione Vaki as the only other back on the roster, creating a clear need for an RB2 to protect Gibbs' workload.

5-time team captain and veteran leader signed 2yr/$17M with Tampa Bay. Was the nominal starter and on-field quarterback of the Lions defense. Detroit now has Jack Campbell and Derrick Barnes as their primary LBs. Anzalone's departure thins an already-light linebacker room.

Signed 2yr/$16M with Washington. Robertson was a valued member of Detroit's 'Legion of Whom' secondary and a versatile slot/boundary corner. His departure thins a CB depth corps that already needs reinforcement but is not a critical starter-level hole given DJ Reed and Ennis Rakestraw.

Signed 2yr/$11.5M with Arizona. Lopez played all 17 games, recording 30 tackles, 2.0 sacks, and 13 pressures in a rotational nose tackle role. His departure alongside DJ Reader still being unsigned leaves Detroit's interior defensive line dangerously thin heading into the draft.

Muhammad had 11.0 sacks in 2025 as the second EDGE opposite Aidan Hutchinson. As of the legal tampering period he remains unsigned. If he departs, Detroit loses their primary pass-rush complement to Hutchinson and must find a new starter. This is the team's biggest remaining defensive need.

Veteran nose tackle is still unsigned as of legal tampering. He has expressed desire to return and the Lions have spoken to his agent, but no deal is done. Has been the run-stuffing anchor of the Lions' defensive interior. His potential departure, on top of losing Lopez, creates a major IDL concern.
Backup swing tackle retired and joined the Lions' coaching staff. Minimal on-field impact but further depletes tackle depth behind a now-vacant LT starter role.
Signed 2yr/$4.1M deal with Buffalo. Was Detroit's backup QB. Need for a backup replacement exists but is not critical given Jared Goff's entrenched starter status.
RFA not tendered by the Lions, making him a free agent. Was developmental depth only. Absence marginally deepens the EDGE need.

signings.analysis: The Lions' headlining free-agency move and their immediate response to losing Graham Glasgow. Mays, 26, was the Panthers' starting center in 2025, allowing zero sacks across 726 snaps and finishing T-9th among qualified centers in PFF's pass-block efficiency metric. The 3-year deal with $14M guaranteed is real investment — he's the unambiguous starter in 2026. At $8.3M AAV, this is mid-tier IOL money, appropriate for a solid-starter-level signing. The IOL need is partially-addressed rather than fully because: (1) the contract's AAV sits at the bottom of the 'starter' tier, leaving room to want a long-term solution; (2) Juice Scruggs is also competing for reps at C/G as a depth piece. IOL drops from Tier 1 critical to Tier 2 — the Lions can still benefit from drafting a developmental center Day 3, but the position is no longer a top-draft priority.

Acquired as part of the David Montgomery trade. Scruggs is a 2023 2nd-round pick from Penn State entering the final year of his rookie deal with $0 guaranteed remaining. He has experience at C, LG, and RG (37 career games, 20 starts). He is a legitimate depth/swing option but cannot be viewed as a starter-quality answer given his inconsistent 2025 performance and the one-year, no-guarantee contract structure. Adds legitimate interior depth and competition without resolving any need on its own.

One-year deal (terms not formally disclosed; prior Dolphins deal was 1yr/$2.5M, similar range expected). Borom is a Detroit native with experience at RT, LT, LG, and RG — 63 games, 38 starts. ESPN notes he is 'best suited as a backup' despite starting experience. At 1-year/depth money, this is a clear bridge/depth signing, not a solution. He replaces Dan Skipper as the swing tackle and provides insurance while the Lions pursue a true starting LT. OT need is NOT meaningfully addressed — this is a minimum-level stabilization. The Lions still must find a starter opposite Sewell via free agency or the draft.
Re-signed via ERFA tender. Bates was one of the Lions' bright spots in 2025. His retention resolves any concern at kicker and was essentially automatic given his exclusive rights status.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Taylor Decker's release (LT) created a starting hole at the most critical position on the offensive line. Detroit had already ranked 31st in pass-block win rate in 2025. Larry Borom's 1-year depth signing is not a starter-level solution. Penei Sewell could slide left, but that creates an RT vacancy. OT is now the top offensive priority in the draft — a top-20 pick at tackle should be strongly considered. Still active in free agency for this position.

Cade Mays (3yr/$25M, $14M gtd) is a legitimate starting center who directly addresses the hole left by Graham Glasgow's release and Frank Ragnow's failed return. Juice Scruggs adds depth. IOL is no longer a critical need. A Day 3 developmental center in the draft would still be welcome, but the position is no longer a top priority.

Al-Quadin Muhammad (11.0 sacks in 2025) remains unsigned. If he departs, the Lions have no proven pass rusher opposite Aidan Hutchinson. Tyrus Wheat was not tendered. Marcus Davenport is also unsigned. Detroit's pass-rush win rate of 32% was near the bottom of the league even WITH Muhammad. EDGE is now the top defensive need and should be addressed via both free agency and the early draft.

The Lions lost both Roy Lopez (2yr/$11.5M to Arizona) and DJ Reader (still unsigned/likely departing) without adding a single interior defensive lineman. Tyleik Williams (2025 1st-round pick) is the only reliable interior piece. Detroit's run defense anchor is gone. IDL should be a Day 2 draft priority and still a free-agency target.

David Montgomery's trade to Houston leaves Jahmyr Gibbs as the only capable back. Sione Vaki is the only other option and is not expected to be an RB2 solution. Detroit needs a power-back complement to protect Gibbs' workload. A mid-tier FA signing or Day 2-3 draft pick is needed.

Alex Anzalone's departure (2yr/$17M to Tampa) leaves Jack Campbell and Derrick Barnes as the primary linebackers. Detroit's LB room is thin for a Dan Campbell defense that relies on physicality. Re-signing depth options (Rodriguez, Stuard, Nowaske) is possible but a veteran addition or Day 3 pick to add depth is warranted.

Amik Robertson's departure (2yr/$16M to Washington) thins the depth behind DJ Reed and Ennis Rakestraw. However, CB remains a Tier 3 need — not critical. The 'Legion of Whom' developmental secondary showed enough promise in 2025 that this is a depth/developmental position, not a priority starter need.

key_additions.headline: 26-year-old center fills the Lions' most pressing offensive need, allowing zero sacks in 726 2025 snaps
Former 2nd-round pick acquired via David Montgomery trade adds interior versatility at C/G
Detroit-native swing tackle replaces Dan Skipper as depth bridge while Lions pursue starting LT
Fan-favorite kicker retained via exclusive rights tender

key_losses.headline: 10-year franchise LT released after contract talks fail, creating the Lions' most urgent draft need
Power-back complement to Gibbs traded away, Detroit now needs an RB2 to manage Gibbs' workload
5-time team captain and defensive leader departs, thinning an already-light linebacker room
Versatile 'Legion of Whom' DB signs with Washington, adding to Detroit's secondary depth concerns
Key rotational NT (2 sacks, 13 pressures in 2025) returns to Arizona, stripping Detroit's IDL depth
Veteran guard/center released after struggling move to center in 2025, accelerating Detroit's IOL rebuild

notes: Legal tampering period opened March 9, 2026. Contracts with Mays, Borom, Anzalone, Robertson, Lopez, and Montgomery/Scruggs trade are all reported by multiple credible sources (NFL Network, ESPN, Detroit News, Pride of Detroit, OverTheCap). Larry Borom's exact contract terms were not publicly disclosed as of March 10 — value estimated at ~$2.5M based on his prior market (1yr/$2.5M with MIA in 2025) and ESPN/NBC characterization as a one-year deal. Juice Scruggs contract value sourced from OverTheCap (final year of 4yr/$6.1M rookie deal, $0 guaranteed remaining). DJ Reader and Al-Quadin Muhammad remain unsigned UFAs as of publish time — their statuses are reflected as departures pending resolution. Cap space figure sourced from OverTheCap real-time tracker ($3,994,788) as of March 10, 2026.
